title: The One Where They Build A House

jokes.text: He even did so for the prison he built and, somewhat ironically, would later occupy.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains that George Sr. always held ribbon-cutting ceremonies for his construction projects.
jokes.punchline: He did so for a prison he built — and then went to prison in it. The word 'ironically' is delivered with total understatement by the narrator.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly compressed dark irony that works on multiple levels: it characterizes George Sr.'s hubris, pays off the narrator's deadpan authority, and uses the single word 'ironically' to do enormous comic lifting. The economy is exceptional — the entire joke lives in one subordinate clause. Earns the 8+ because it's the kind of dense, effortlessly elegant joke that defines the show's voice.

jokes.text: There's a cream with real diamonds in it. I can actually smear diamonds on my face, and it's only $400 a tub.
jokes.setup: Michael is presenting his serious business model to save the family company.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay completely ignores him and starts marveling at diamond face cream, the price of which she considers a bargain.
jokes.explanation: Classic Lindsay obliviousness — her self-absorption is so total that she tunes out a family crisis to discuss face cream. The character integration is strong; this could only be Lindsay. The comedy is functional and in-character but not particularly inventive in its construction.

jokes.text: That's like, what, like a million diamonds for $400? A million- diamonds.
jokes.setup: Lindsay brags about $400 diamond face cream as a great deal.
jokes.punchline: Tobias attempts to do the math with child-like, awe-struck credulity, arriving at 'a million diamonds' as if this is a genuine calculation and repeating it in disbelief at the value.
jokes.explanation: The comic rhythm of the repeated 'a million diamonds' captures Tobias's specific brand of dense sincerity. It's funny in performance but fairly thin on the page — it relies almost entirely on delivery.

jokes.text: My husband's jealous because since we decided to have an open marriage, I've been doing a little better than he has.
jokes.setup: Tobias asks if Lindsay is on him about the diamond cream, and Lindsay launches into a confessional.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay is boasting about doing better in their open marriage — but the immediate context makes clear neither of them is actually doing anything. The irony is she's completely deluded about her own success.
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the dramatic irony the narrator will immediately undercut. Lindsay's competitive framing of their marriage is very in-character. Works as setup for a bigger joke more than as a standalone.

jokes.text: I should be telling you that I have been meeting my fair share of groupies. Or should I say 'Bluepies'?
jokes.setup: Tobias is boasting about meeting groupies in the context of their open marriage.
jokes.punchline: 'Bluepies' — a pun on 'groupies' fused with his blue body paint from auditioning for the Blue Man Group. The wordplay is also a piece of self-delusion: his 'groupies' are fellow Blue Man Group auditioners.
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in Arrested Development's layered comedy. The joke works on three simultaneous levels: (1) a groan-worthy but technically clever pun, (2) character comedy — only Tobias would coin this word with this degree of pride, (3) dramatic irony — the 'groupies' are other Blue Man Group auditioners, not romantic conquests. The character integration score is near-perfect: 'Bluepies' is a word that could ONLY exist in Tobias's mouth. The subsequent narrator clarification makes it even better. Earns the 8+.

jokes.text: Tobias's Blue Man Group audition: 'And this is Kids from Bye Bye Birdie' — performing silently in blue paint for a silent performance art trio.
jokes.setup: Tobias auditioned for the Blue Man Group, which is a silent performance art trio.
jokes.punchline: Tobias is performing a sung number — 'Kids' from Bye Bye Birdie — loudly, for an audition where silence is the entire point.
jokes.explanation: Inferred visual gag. The comedy is in the conceptual absurdity — Tobias, the failed actor, fundamentally misunderstands the nature of a silent performance art group and performs a sung Broadway number. It's beautifully stupid in a way that is entirely character-specific. The joke exists mostly as a visual/contextual gag inferred from the narrator setup.

jokes.text: In fact, neither Lindsay nor Tobias did have the guts to go through with it. [Cut to both claiming they already have.]
jokes.setup: The narrator questions whether either has the guts for an open marriage; both immediately and simultaneously claim they have.
jokes.punchline: The narrator has already told us they haven't, so the cut to their confident mutual claims lands as pure, well-structured dramatic irony.
jokes.explanation: The show's signature move — narrator undercuts the character before they even speak. The structural cleanness of the edit is what elevates this above a standard dramatic irony beat. It's economical and sharp, a perfect example of the show's mockumentary-adjacent layering.

jokes.text: Lindsay tries to buy a drink for a man at the bar; he immediately leaves. Then a second man offers to buy her one; she turns him down.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains Lindsay has lost her self-confidence. She tries to pick up a man; meanwhile Tobias secretly watches.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay fails to attract the man she approaches, and the narrator reveals Tobias is secretly keeping tabs on her — the 'open marriage' is a total pantomime on both sides.
jokes.explanation: Solid situational comedy establishing the Lindsay/Tobias dynamic, but mostly serves as setup for later payoffs. The double beat (her striking out, then rejecting someone herself) adds texture but isn't a standout moment.

jokes.text: Can I bring Ann? / Who?
jokes.setup: George Michael asks if he can bring his girlfriend Ann to the ribbon-cutting.
jokes.punchline: Michael's blank 'Who?' establishes the running gag of the entire Bluth family being unable to register Ann's existence.
jokes.explanation: The launch of one of Arrested Development's great multi-season running gags. The joke's economy is perfect — one word ('Who?') does everything. It's funny on first viewing as a rude dismissal, and exponentially funnier on rewatch knowing how developed this gag becomes. The 'Her?' variant later in the same episode shows the show immediately starting to escalate and vary the gag. Earns 8 for economy alone, plus the cultural weight of the running gag.

jokes.text: She's got a little hard-boiled egg going there.
jokes.setup: Michael finally spots Ann and acknowledges knowing her.
jokes.punchline: His description of Ann's snack — a hard-boiled egg — delivered with bemused deadpan, begins the show's sustained joke about Ann's profound plainness.
jokes.explanation: The hard-boiled egg as Ann's defining accessory is a perfect piece of characterization-via-prop comedy. It says everything about Ann's personality without saying anything explicitly mean. The casual 'going there' as if it's a fashion accessory is a nice touch of Michael's voice.

jokes.text: She sometimes takes a little pack of mayonnaise and she'll squirt it in her mouth, and then she'll take an egg and kind of — mmm. She calls it a mayonn-egg.
jokes.setup: Michael's affectionate but slightly nauseated observation about Ann's egg.
jokes.punchline: George Michael lovingly describes Ann's signature snack — squirting mayo directly in her mouth and adding an egg — with complete sincerity. He's named the combination. Michael nearly gets sick.
jokes.explanation: A sublime piece of cringe comedy. The 'mayonn-egg' joke works because George Michael's total sincerity makes it both sweet and nauseating simultaneously. The portmanteau word is inventive, the physical detail (squirting mayo directly in mouth) is genuinely revolting, and Michael's 'Are you okay?' in response reveals the joke has genuinely affected him. The character integration is exceptional — George Michael's innocent pride in Ann's weird snack says everything about his earnest, uncool romantic attachment. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Are you okay? / I don't feel so good.
jokes.setup: George Michael describes the mayonn-egg in loving detail.
jokes.punchline: Michael, a grown adult, is physically unsettled by hearing about a condiment snack. He admits it: 'I don't feel so good.'
jokes.explanation: The perfect capper to the mayonn-egg bit. Michael's understated admission that he's actually been affected is a nice payoff. Slightly elevated by being the punchline of the larger mayonn-egg sequence rather than a standalone.

jokes.text: George Michael wants to buy Ann some diamond cream. / Her?
jokes.setup: George Michael reveals he wants to spend money on a gift for Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Her?' — a variation on the 'Who?' from earlier — continues the running gag. The single-word incredulity is perfectly timed.
jokes.explanation: The variation from 'Who?' to 'Her?' is a sign of great running gag craft — the gag escalates and shifts slightly each time. 'Her?' has become one of the most quoted lines in Arrested Development's history, and with good reason: one syllable, maximum contempt, perfect comedic economy. This is already approaching cultural-touchstone territory within the same episode.

jokes.text: I'm sure that Egg is a very nice person.
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael not to spend money on Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael refers to Ann as 'Egg' — a new nickname that accidentally but perfectly describes her personality. He doesn't even realize he's done it.
jokes.explanation: The accidental nickname 'Egg' is born here, and it's a perfect piece of organic joke construction — Michael has been looking at Ann's egg, and it has unconsciously merged with his perception of her. The fact that he says it with complete sincerity ('a very nice person') while getting her name wrong is layered character comedy. This gag too will run for seasons. Earns the high score for organic invention and character integration.

jokes.text: It's Ann. Her na- / all your money getting her all glittered up for Easter.
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to correct Michael's use of 'Egg' as Ann's name.
jokes.punchline: Michael plows right through the correction with a non-sequitur about Easter, which is both a dig at Ann's religiosity and evidence that he has completely not registered the correction.
jokes.explanation: The Easter reference is a quick, sharp jab at Ann's religious conservatism that rewards rewatching (it becomes a bigger theme) while also functioning as a simple dismissal. Michael's refusal to even acknowledge the name correction is good character work.

jokes.text: Hey, Dad may have illegally built some homes in Iraq, which is kinda not cool.
jokes.setup: Gob is defending himself as president while Michael explains the company's image problem.
jokes.punchline: Gob summarizes the serious criminal allegation — building homes in Iraq for Saddam Hussein — as 'kinda not cool,' a catastrophic understatement that also reveals his total moral vacuity.
jokes.explanation: Gob's moral compass rendered as 'kinda not cool' is a perfect character-integrated understatement. The phrase 'kinda not cool' for treason-adjacent war profiteering is funny precisely because it's the language of mild social disapproval applied to serious crime. Classic Gob.

jokes.text: If you had a business model, then by all means, go in there and do your little dance.
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob are arguing about who gets to present the business plan to the board.
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'do your little dance' is Michael's sarcastic dismissal — but the show will literally deliver Gob doing a dance in front of the board moments later, making this inadvertent foreshadowing.
jokes.explanation: Functions as inadvertent setup for the 'Solid as a Rock' boardroom dance later. On first viewing it's a decent sarcastic line; on rewatch it's a perfect piece of planted comedy. Moderate standalone, elevated by what follows.

jokes.text: Rock, paper, scissors for it. / No, no, I'm not interested in doing— / One, two, three. / Paper covers rock. / It is a rock though. It should beat everything.
jokes.setup: Gob proposes rock paper scissors to decide who presents to the board.
jokes.punchline: Michael refuses; Gob does it anyway unilaterally and wins. Michael then challenges the fundamental logic of rock-paper-scissors, arguing a rock should beat everything.
jokes.explanation: The running rock-paper-scissors gag is established here. Michael's logic — 'It is a rock though. It should beat everything' — is the kind of absurdist literalism that pays off spectacularly at the episode's climax. Great structure for a running gag, with strong character integration: Michael is a man who believes in real-world logic over arbitrary convention.

jokes.text: There's not a lot of logic to it. It's like on a boat with 'Women and children first.' Why should they—
jokes.setup: Michael defends his 'rock beats everything' logic.
jokes.punchline: Michael's argument spirals into questioning 'women and children first' on a sinking boat — which then transitions him directly into announcing he's buying a boat, making it a perfect non-sequitur pivot.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed transition joke. The pivot from abstract logic (rock paper scissors) to 'women and children first' to abruptly announcing a boat purchase is a sharp structural comedic move. It reveals Gob's true agenda mid-philosophical tangent. The 'which, before I forget' is a perfect comic pivot line.

jokes.text: Hello. / Hi. / What a beauty. / Thank you. / I meant you. / Well.
jokes.setup: Gob approaches what appears to be a woman at a boat show.
jokes.punchline: His 'What a beauty' seems like a compliment to the woman, who thanks him — then he reveals he meant the boat. The saleswoman's 'Well.' is a perfect deadpan non-reaction.
jokes.explanation: A classic misdirection joke — the subverted compliment. The structure is clean and economical, but the core joke (compliment meant for object, not person) is not particularly original. The saleswoman's 'Well.' is the best part. Functional.

jokes.text: Let's get some digits. / I'm sorry. I don't give it out without a firm offer. / Oh, I'll make you a firm offer. / Unfortunately, she wasn't flirting.
jokes.setup: Gob asks for the saleswoman's phone number.
jokes.punchline: 'A firm offer' is boat-sale language (a formal purchase offer), not romantic. Gob misreads professional sales jargon as flirtation. The narrator's flat 'Unfortunately, she wasn't flirting' is the perfect dry capper.
jokes.explanation: A smartly constructed double-meaning joke. 'A firm offer' works perfectly in both contexts (romantic and real estate/boat sales), and Gob's oblivious confidence in his own charm is pure character comedy. The narrator's deadpan correction is essential to the punchline landing fully.

jokes.text: The Seaward. / You're not getting a boat. / One, two, three— / No, not gonna do it. / Paper covers rock. / Fine. But rock sinks boat.
jokes.setup: Gob names the boat 'The Seaward' and tries to play rock paper scissors again.
jokes.punchline: Michael refuses rock-paper-scissors this time, then invents 'rock sinks boat' as a new rule — escalating the game's logic absurdly. Meanwhile 'The Seaward' sounds exactly like 'The C-word,' setting up the Lucille callback.
jokes.explanation: Densely layered. 'The Seaward' is a brilliant near-homophone pun (sounds like 'the C-word') that pays off immediately in the Lucille scene and rewards rewatch with the awareness that Gob named his boat something that sounds obscene. 'Rock sinks boat' escalates the rock-paper-scissors absurdist logic perfectly. Two simultaneous jokes firing at maximum compression. Among the episode's best constructed moments.

jokes.text: Get rid of the Seaward. / I'll leave when I'm good and ready.
jokes.setup: Lucille walks in and tells Michael to get rid of 'the Seaward' (the boat).
jokes.punchline: Gob hears it as 'Get rid of the C-word' — an instruction to leave — and responds as if she's told him to get out. The homophonic misunderstanding makes Lucille's innocent sentence sound like a personal insult.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's crown jewel and one of Arrested Development's most celebrated jokes. The construction is flawless: Gob names the boat 'The Seaward' (sounds like 'the C-word') → Lucille innocently says 'Get rid of the Seaward' → Gob hears 'Get rid of the C-word' and takes personal offense. The joke required two setups (the boat name AND Lucille's entrance) firing simultaneously. It's structurally airtight, family-friendly by technicality, maximally quotable, and gets funnier every rewatch. Calibrated against the 9.5 anchor: this is comparable quality — it's a cultural touchstone of the show that people cite by name. Earns the top score.

jokes.text: Oscar, we'll be heard. / That's what makes it so hot.
jokes.setup: Michael hears strange noises from his mother's apartment and investigates.
jokes.punchline: Lucille whispers that they'll be heard; Oscar responds that that's what makes it hot — revealing they're having a secret affair, which the audience now understands from context.
jokes.explanation: Effective reveal of the Oscar/Lucille affair through overheard dialogue. The 'that's what makes it so hot' line has decent character work for Oscar's free-spirit personality, but the joke itself (overhearing a sexual encounter) is fairly standard. Works well in context.

jokes.text: I heard zoo noises. It's nothing. Go back to bed. / So, everybody's still up, huh?
jokes.setup: Lucille tries to cover for the sounds of her affair with Oscar; Michael investigates.
jokes.punchline: Oscar, completely oblivious to the need for discretion, cheerfully wanders into the scene with 'So, everybody's still up, huh?' — the most incriminating possible thing to say.
jokes.explanation: Oscar's guileless, post-coital cheerfulness ('So, everybody's still up, huh?') is perfectly in character — he has absolutely no instinct for concealment. The 'zoo noises' cover from Lucille makes the subsequent reveal even more awkward. Good character-integrated cringe comedy.

jokes.text: You volunteered him. / I knew you were going to throw that in my face.
jokes.setup: Lucille claims it's not fair that Buster is going to the army.
jokes.punchline: Michael points out she volunteered him; Lucille's response — 'I knew you were going to throw that in my face' — treats her own act of volunteering her son for military service as a minor conversational unfairness.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's self-victimhood is perfectly captured here — she volunteered her son for war and is already positioning Michael's accurate recollection as a personal attack on her. Peak Lucille Bluth character comedy.

jokes.text: Don't worry about it, okay? They're never gonna take him anyway.
jokes.setup: Michael reassures Lucille that Buster won't be accepted by the army.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately cuts to Buster at the recruitment center, listing his numerous disqualifying medical conditions — which the army accepts anyway.
jokes.explanation: Standard dramatic irony setup — the show immediately undermines Michael's confidence. Works mainly as a transition into the Buster physical comedy sequence rather than as a strong standalone joke.

jokes.text: Buster's army physical: 'My reproductive organ is shaped like a lobster tail, but without its shell, hmm?' / 'I guess I have the panic attacks under control.' / 'I'm legally blind at night.'
jokes.setup: Buster is undergoing his army physical, listing his many medical conditions.
jokes.punchline: The list is an escalating catalog of increasingly bizarre and specific medical issues, delivered with Buster's signature anxious earnestness. The lobster-tail comparison is the peak absurdist detail.
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of Buster's infirmities is a showcase of character-specific absurdist comedy. 'My reproductive organ is shaped like a lobster tail, but without its shell, hmm?' is a completely unexpected, deeply specific detail delivered with Buster's characteristic anxious deference ('hmm?'). The escalation from standard conditions (panic attacks) to the genuinely surreal (lobster tail) is perfectly structured. 'I'm legally blind at night' as the closing item — delivered as if it's an afterthought — is excellent. Character integration near-perfect.

jokes.text: Okay then, let's get you fitted for a uniform. / What? What?
jokes.setup: Buster has confidently listed every possible disqualifying condition he has.
jokes.punchline: The recruiter immediately accepts him. Buster's 'What? What?' captures the exact shock of someone whose careful self-sabotage has completely failed.
jokes.explanation: The punchline — recruiter unfazed — is the payoff to all of Buster's listed conditions. The 'What? What?' double is a perfect Buster reaction: he's genuinely shocked that the army wants a man who can't see at night and has a lobster-shaped reproductive organ. The narrator's 'Buster had miscalculated the army's current need for personnel' is a superb dry capper.

jokes.text: Hey, why don't you volunteer Oscar? That would solve both of your problems. / He'd never cut his hair. / That hair.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests volunteering Oscar for the army to get him out of Lucille's life.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's only objection to sending her lover to war is his hair. Her wistful 'That hair' at the end suggests genuine conflicted attraction.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's priorities — her lover's hair over her son's military safety — are perfectly in character. The compressed 'That hair.' as a moment of involuntary longing is the punch. Good economy.

jokes.text: I keep eating so many eggs. It must be my unconscious desire to have a baby.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Kitty are hiding in Mexico; Kitty is eating eggs.
jokes.punchline: Kitty's logic — eggs = unconscious desire to have a baby with George Sr. — is a non-sequitur that also serves as a threat. The 'unconscious' qualifier implies she thinks she's being scientific.
jokes.explanation: Kitty's egg-eating logic is funny because of the absurd pseudo-psychology, but also because it foreshadows the Ann/mayonn-egg joke earlier in the episode — both involve women and eggs in comedically charged situations. Moderate joke, decent character work.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Can you even imagine how cute the combination of the two of us would be?
jokes.setup: Kitty muses about having George Sr.'s baby.
jokes.punchline: The audience knows George Sr. is a fugitive criminal hiding in Mexico — her daydream about their combined attractiveness is delusional and disturbing in equal measure.
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy — Kitty's delusion about the romance is in-character but the joke itself is fairly standard 'deluded character talks about fantasy future.' Does some character work but isn't inventive.

jokes.text: We are all out of 'prophylacticos' anyway. / Somebody used the last two on his feet to walk across the bathroom floor.
jokes.setup: Kitty suggests they might as well try to have a baby since they're out of condoms.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reveals he used the last two condoms as foot coverings to walk across the bathroom floor — a piece of extreme germophobia/poverty-of-options that's simultaneously gross and pathetic.
jokes.explanation: The specific detail of using condoms as bathroom-floor foot covers is a great piece of absurdist character comedy — it reveals both the squalor of their hideout and George Sr.'s particular brand of fastidiousness even in fugitive conditions. 'Prophylacticos' as the Spanglish term is a nice touch.

jokes.text: I've made a huge mistake.
jokes.setup: George Sr. reflects on his situation hiding in Mexico with Kitty.
jokes.punchline: 'I've made a huge mistake' is the Bluth family's signature line of resigned self-awareness, delivered here by George Sr. The understatement (he's a fugitive criminal; his mistake is Kitty) is perfectly calibrated.
jokes.explanation: 'I've made a huge mistake' is the show's most iconic recurring line, and this may be its origin point (or near it). Maximum economy — four words capturing a character's entire existential situation. The line will become a touchstone for the series and entered popular culture. High quotability.

jokes.text: You're the only one who can save the family, but you gotta keep the company strong, because I'm gonna need some of that money later.
jokes.setup: George Sr. makes a secret phone call to Michael from Mexico, ostensibly to offer paternal guidance.
jokes.punchline: His 'save the family' speech immediately reveals itself to be self-interested — he needs the company strong for his own financial benefit. The 'because I'm gonna need some of that money later' is delivered without irony.
jokes.explanation: Classic George Sr. — the apparent paternal moment curdles immediately into financial self-interest. The pivot from 'you're the only one who can save the family' to 'I'm gonna need that money' is rapid and perfectly characterizes the man. Good character work.

jokes.text: I called to talk to Michael, you horse's—
jokes.setup: Gob has intercepted the phone call from George Sr. to Michael and has been taking credit.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. calls Gob a 'horse's ___' — the sentence is cut off before the expletive by Gob hanging up, but the meaning is unambiguous. The edit is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The cut-off insult is a standard TV comedy technique but it's well-executed here. The setup (Gob has intercepted Dad's call to Michael) makes the 'horse's—' feel earned as a character moment rather than a cheap bleep-joke.

jokes.text: Gob was hurt. / Bye, Pop. / Hey, whoa, whoa — was that Dad? / Yeah, I had to, uh, jump off.
jokes.setup: George Sr. called specifically for Michael; Gob intercepted and got called a 'horse's ass.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator says 'Gob was hurt' — and indeed, Gob IS hurt, but only because his father insulted him, not because he realizes he just denied Michael a call from their father.
jokes.explanation: The triple wound — George Sr. insults Gob, Gob denies Michael the call, and Michael is also wounded — is efficiently conveyed through the narrator's 'Gob was hurt' and Michael's 'was that Dad?' The structure layers three emotional beats in seconds.

jokes.text: No way to tell, really. Portugal. Down South America way.
jokes.setup: Michael asks where their father was calling from.
jokes.punchline: Gob's geographic confusion — Portugal is not in South America — is delivered with complete confidence. 'Down South America way' as a vague directional is perfect Gob idiom.
jokes.explanation: Gob's confident geographic ignorance is a reliable character note — he says 'Portugal, down South America way' with the authority of someone who has no idea and has decided to anyway. Classic Gob.

jokes.text: Did he wanna talk to me? / No, he wanted to talk to the president. / I have no problem with that.
jokes.setup: Michael discovers Gob intercepted his father's call.
jokes.punchline: Gob tells Michael their father wanted to talk to 'the president' — and Michael says 'I have no problem with that,' his running-gag denial of wounded pride. The audience knows he does have a problem with it.
jokes.explanation: 'I have no problem with that' is Michael's running self-deception line throughout the episode. Here it's deployed at its most poignant — he absolutely has a problem with his father not wanting to talk to him — making it simultaneously funny and sad.

jokes.text: George Sr. was brutally hurt when he was mistaken for his twin brother Oscar. / Marijuana? No, no. That's my brother. 'Brothero. Brothero.'
jokes.setup: The narrator does a symmetry beat — Gob was hurt, Michael was hurt, and now George Sr. was hurt.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s hurt is because Mexican police think he's Oscar (due to marijuana) — and his attempt to clarify in Spanglish ('Brothero! Brothero!') is both desperate and revealing (he's clearly in police custody).
jokes.explanation: The triptych of 'hurt' characters (Gob, Michael, George Sr.) is a structurally elegant comedy device — each hurt for a different reason, each self-centered. 'Brothero! Brothero!' as George Sr.'s panicked Spanglish is a good absurdist detail.

jokes.text: Lindsay spots a man with 'lean look, effortless hair, and dressed-down manner of a movie star' — who is later revealed to be an actual homeless man.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is at a club, having struck out. She spots a man who looks like a movie star.
jokes.punchline: The man looks like a movie star because he's living rough — he's a homeless man (later revealed to be an actual movie star doing research). Lindsay's attraction is based on a total misread.
jokes.explanation: The Tom Jane plot is a beautifully structured slow-burn misdirection — Lindsay thinks she's found a cool bohemian; he's homeless; he's actually a movie star slumming it for research. The layers reward rewatching. This initial beat sets up the triple twist.

jokes.text: Uh, uh, uh, you get out! Get out, out, out! No homeless in here! / Homeless? You're homeless? / You're gross. Oh, God.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has arranged to meet the attractive man she's been flirting with.
jokes.punchline: A bouncer throws him out for being homeless — Lindsay, moments ago attracted to him, immediately pivots to 'You're gross. Oh God.' Her disgust is instant and total, which is her most savage character note.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's immediate flip from 'this mysterious man' to 'you're gross, oh God' is a great character beat — her shallowness is total and instantaneous. Also sets up the later beat where she has to grovel back to him for her 'charitable' gesture.

jokes.text: They didn't think there was enough wrong with me. / Well, did they check everything? / Yes. They even touched my Charlie Browns.
jokes.setup: Buster reports that the army accepted him despite his catalog of conditions.
jokes.punchline: 'They even touched my Charlie Browns' — Buster's childlike euphemism for his genitals is both funny and deeply uncomfortable, especially to Lucille, his overbearing mother.
jokes.explanation: 'Charlie Browns' is an inspired piece of character writing — it's exactly the word a man-child raised by an emotionally suffocating mother would use for his anatomy in front of her. The specific choice of 'Charlie Browns' (slightly sad, round, comic strip-inflected) is perfect. This could only come from Buster. High character integration score.

jokes.text: Now, which pinkie do you use less?
jokes.setup: Lucille is desperately trying to get Buster out of army service.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's solution is to have Buster deliberately injure a pinkie finger — the specific logic of 'which do you use less?' is both extremely dark (maiming your child) and extremely practical (she's thought about this).
jokes.explanation: The specific word 'pinkie' and the utilitarian framing ('which do you use less?') is what makes this land. Lucille has casually arrived at deliberate self-harm as a parenting solution, and the economy of the question reveals how well she's considered it. Strong character integration — this is peak Lucille: loving, controlling, and clinically pragmatic about injury.

jokes.text: Are you nuts? Do you know what I went through to have this boy? / Oh, I know where the boy came from.
jokes.setup: Lucille is outraged at the suggestion of injuring Buster's finger.
jokes.punchline: Oscar's 'Oh, I know where the boy came from' implies he may be Buster's biological father — a long-running ambiguity in the series. Delivered with complete calm.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly compressed delivery of one of the show's ongoing mysteries (Oscar as Buster's real father). The line does enormous work in six words — rewatch bonus because its full weight only lands on subsequent viewings when the show has further developed the Buster paternity subplot.

jokes.text: Wait. Why were you in the pantry?
jokes.setup: Oscar has just implied knowledge of Buster's parentage.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's first response is not to address the paternity implication but to ask why he was in the pantry — suggesting the comment came from a specific location that raises its own questions.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection to pantry logistics is a classic Arrested Development move — answering a charged moment with a completely mundane non-sequitur that implies a whole other scandal. Why WAS he in the pantry? It's the right question. Lucille's priorities are perfectly backwards.

jokes.text: I said, 'That's why you're no longer president.' [Board immediately promotes timeline to two weeks.]
jokes.setup: Michael explains his two-month timeline to the board. Gob is present as the 'president.'
jokes.punchline: Gob apparently made an offhand insult to Michael in front of the board ('That's why you're no longer president') and the board, taking it as presidential authority, cut the timeline to two weeks. We only see the aftermath, not the incident — which is funnier.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because we don't see it happen — the comedy is in the compressed aftermath. Gob accidentally creating a crisis with an off-the-cuff remark is perfect character work. The board's deference to the 'president' title regardless of his competence is a nice satirical note.

jokes.text: Two weeks. Let's do it in two weeks. Hey! [Music cue, dancing]
jokes.setup: Gob has just cut Michael's timeline in half with a careless remark.
jokes.punchline: Gob celebrates his own blunder by calling for music and dancing — completely unaware he's made things worse. The dancing follows Michael's earlier 'do your little dance' line.
jokes.explanation: Gob's celebratory dance in the boardroom is a callback payoff to Michael's sarcastic 'do your little dance' from earlier — the show made good on a seemingly throwaway line. Gob's obliviousness to his own destructiveness is perfectly rendered in his instinct to celebrate.

jokes.text: Meet Starla, the new Bluth Company business model for our phase two campaign. / Solid as a rock.
jokes.setup: Michael challenged Gob to produce a 'business model' if he wants to run things.
jokes.punchline: Gob produces a literal 'business model' — a beautiful woman named Starla — playing on the double meaning of 'model.' The Ashford & Simpson song 'Solid as a Rock' plays as Gob's chosen theme.
jokes.explanation: The 'business model' wordplay is elegant — Gob interprets 'business model' as 'model who works for a business,' which is both deeply stupid and entirely in character. The 'Solid as a Rock' song choice as a corporate theme is a perfect absurdist touch. This joke pays off again at the ribbon-cutting when the house literally fails to be solid.

jokes.text: We can't build a house in two weeks. Also, I'm not sure how 'Solid as a Rock' helps people forget the fact that we built houses in Iraq.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to assess the damage from Gob's boardroom performance.
jokes.punchline: Michael notes that 'Solid as a Rock' is a bad slogan for a company whose crimes involved building (in Iraq), not for any structural concern. The specific complaint about the Iraq-construction optics is a sharp observational beat.
jokes.explanation: Michael's critique is sharp — 'Solid as a Rock' is a slogan chosen for purely aesthetic/romantic reasons that happens to be tone-deaf to the company's actual scandal. A good observational beat that characterizes both Gob's obliviousness and Michael's overthinking.

jokes.text: We don't have to build a real house, Michael. Like you said, it's all for appearances anyway. We throw up a couple of walls. / Nothing on the inside.
jokes.setup: Michael worries about building a house in two weeks.
jokes.punchline: Lucille proposes a completely fraudulent facade — 'nothing on the inside' — as a totally rational business solution. Her casualness about committing new fraud while already under investigation for fraud is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's 'nothing on the inside' solution is perfectly in character — she lives in a world of surfaces and appearances, literally and figuratively. The proposal is both satirically resonant (the family has always been a facade) and darkly funny as straight-faced fraud advice.

jokes.text: Sorry about that 'build it yourself' crap. I might take her to the ribbon-cutting.
jokes.setup: Gob has just issued demands; he now appears to apologize.
jokes.punchline: His 'apology' is immediately followed by his plan to bring Starla (the business model) to the ribbon-cutting — so the apology is just a segue into his next selfish interest.
jokes.explanation: A good Gob character beat — his apologies are always transitional, never genuine. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Guess who was just over her? Don't, I'll tell you — me. / L— the business model. / Actually they just made out. / Yeah, she had all kinds of orgasms.
jokes.setup: Michael calls to get Starla's number. Gob answers.
jokes.punchline: Gob's escalating oversharing about his sexual encounter with Starla — unprompted, in detail, culminating in 'she had all kinds of orgasms' — is a sustained cringe moment. He's performing for Michael rather than talking to him.
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-aggrandizing sexual oversharing is very in-character. The escalation from 'was just over her' to specific orgasm commentary is a good comedic rhythm, though the joke relies somewhat on shock value rather than structural elegance.

jokes.text: Did you see the news? Your Uncle Oscar forced himself on me at the beach today and I didn't have my horn.
jokes.setup: Michael calls his mother; she's seen the news about the Oscar/beach incident.
jokes.punchline: Lucille reframes the consensual romantic encounter as Oscar forcing himself on her, and the detail 'I didn't have my horn' (a rape whistle? a car horn?) is both nonsensical and perfectly Lucille-ian.
jokes.explanation: 'I didn't have my horn' is a brilliantly specific absurd detail. Whether it means a car horn, an air horn, or a rape whistle, the image of Lucille carrying a horn for emergencies is perfect character comedy. Her immediate revision of a consensual tryst into a forced encounter is both dark and exactly how Lucille processes inconvenient realities.

jokes.text: He looked like a movie star because he was a movie star. His name was Tom Jane, and he was making two movies for a major studio. One was a gritty personal project about a junkie's life, which he got to do in exchange for making a rigidly, formulaic popcorn movie.
jokes.setup: Lindsay thought Tom Jane looked like a movie star. He IS one.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan description of the Hollywood quid-pro-quo (gritty personal project in exchange for formulaic popcorn movie) is a sharp industry observation delivered as neutral fact.
jokes.explanation: The Hollywood creative deal parody is a sharp insider joke delivered with complete narrator deadpan. The specificity ('rigidly, formulaic') is funny because it's so accurate. Also works as a meta-joke about the kind of prestige TV vs. commercial TV tradeoffs that Arrested Development itself navigated.

jokes.text: Well, at first I thought you were kind of hot, but I hadn't looked closely enough.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is apologizing to Tom Jane for freaking out about him being 'homeless.'
jokes.punchline: Her apology essentially says: 'You're actually ugly when I look closely.' It's both insulting and completely oblivious.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's 'apology' is worse than the original offense — calling him ugly while apologizing for her shallowness. Only Lindsay could deliver this with complete sincerity. The character integration is very high: this is a deeply Lindsay-specific way of saying the wrong thing.

jokes.text: I just want my kids back. [The mystery fifth voice during the crew motivation scene]
jokes.setup: Michael asks why everyone wants to build the house. We've heard from Oscar, George Michael, Buster. Then:
jokes.punchline: A fifth voice — presumably a random construction worker or Tom Jane — simply says 'I just want my kids back,' which is an entirely different kind of desperation from everyone else's.
jokes.explanation: One of the funniest non-sequitur beats in the episode. The complete tonal shift — from playful motivations to raw custody battle desperation — from an unnamed character lands with perfect timing. The economy is perfect: five words that are both wholly unexpected and somehow completely believable as a background character's reality. Classic AD layering of throwaway background comedy.

jokes.text: Imagine that. I finally nailed somebody that you weren't after.
jokes.setup: Gob is bragging about his conquest of Starla.
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals that his primary motive for sleeping with Starla was competitive — it's about finally besting Michael in romance. The tragedy is that Michael wasn't interested in Starla at all, making Gob's 'victory' hollow.
jokes.explanation: A good revelation of Gob's competitive psychology — his romantic life is about beating Michael, not genuine attraction. The irony (Michael wasn't after Starla) enriches the moment.

jokes.text: I built that company, that house, and I'm gonna be cutting the ribbon whether we're all pretending that you're president or not.
jokes.setup: Michael has finally snapped at Gob's relentless credit-stealing.
jokes.punchline: The admission 'whether we're all pretending that you're president or not' is the show casually acknowledging the entire corporate fiction it's been building. The phrase 'pretending' is doing all the work.
jokes.explanation: Michael's outburst punctures the company's fiction with one word — 'pretending.' It's a good dramatic beat with comedic payoff in its honesty. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I just wanted you to be impressed with me. / Dad never was. Never said a nice thing about me.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Gob about constantly stealing credit.
jokes.punchline: Gob's sudden vulnerability — 'I just wanted you to be impressed with me. Dad never was' — is a comedic pivot into genuine pathos that's more affecting than funny, but works as character comedy in its unexpectedness.
jokes.explanation: Arrested Development's ability to pivot from comedy to genuine emotion without losing tone is on display here. Gob's confession is unexpected and character-deepening. It's less a 'joke' than a tonal beat, but it earns a laugh of recognition and sets up the emotional brother moment.

jokes.text: Well, actually, I figured, give Gob a chance, you know? The guy doesn't get a whole lot of attention in this family.
jokes.setup: It's the big ribbon-cutting day. Michael says he's letting Gob cut the ribbon.
jokes.punchline: Michael's justification — 'doesn't get a lot of attention' — is deeply ironic given that Gob is the most attention-seeking member of an extremely attention-seeking family.
jokes.explanation: A nice ironic reversal — Michael's generous framing is the opposite of reality. Functions more as setup for the ribbon-cutting scene than as a strong joke in itself.

jokes.text: My brother wasn't optimistic it could be done. But I didn't take 'wasn't optimistic it could be done' for an answer.
jokes.setup: Gob introduces the ribbon-cutting ceremony to the assembled crowd.
jokes.punchline: 'I didn't take wasn't optimistic it could be done for an answer' is Gob's mangled version of 'I didn't take no for an answer.' The grammatical collapse of this idiom is an in-character malapropism.
jokes.explanation: Gob's speech-making is a rich vein of comedy — he's confident and pompous but grammatically challenged. The specific mangling of 'I didn't take no for an answer' into a dependent clause that barely makes sense is excellent in-character wordplay. It's also subtly ironic because Michael did all the actual work.

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, I present to you phase two of the Bluth Company. A house truly solid as a rock. [House immediately collapses]
jokes.setup: Gob cuts the ribbon on the fake house, delivering a speech about its solidity, using 'Solid as a Rock' as the slogan.
jokes.punchline: The house immediately collapses. 'Solid as a Rock' has been the episode's running slogan and the house literally cannot support itself. The collapse is the culmination of every fake-construction joke in the episode.
jokes.explanation: The house collapse is the episode's structural climax — every plot thread (fake construction, 'Solid as a Rock' slogan, Gob's desire for credit, Michael's two-week timeline, Lucille's 'nothing on the inside' advice) converges in one physical comedy moment. The structure score is near-perfect because this is textbook earned physical comedy: the audience has been told the house is fake, told it would look good but be empty, watched it be built by amateurs, and now it collapses at exactly the moment of maximum public exposure. The joke is the entire episode's thesis made physical.

jokes.text: But as it turns out, Michael was right to not be optimistic it could be done. [The house collapses]
jokes.setup: Gob has just finished his triumphant speech about the house.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan callback to the 'optimistic' phrasing plays as the house presumably collapses.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the speech is structurally excellent — the narrator mirrors Gob's exact clunky phrasing back at the moment of disaster. The economy is outstanding: the narrator says everything by saying almost nothing. The structure of the callback (same awkward phrase, now true in the worst way) earns a high score.

jokes.text: Buster saw the chance to get injured enough to keep him out of the army — but instead got a glimpse inside.
jokes.setup: Buster has been looking for ways to hurt himself to avoid military service.
jokes.punchline: The house collapses, and Buster's chance to be injured is right there — but instead of getting hurt, he only sees the (presumably scandalous) inside of the house. The specific disappointment of 'only got a glimpse inside' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured: Buster's self-injury plotting throughout the episode (looking for ways to hurt himself at the construction site) leads to a moment of perfect opportunity that he completely fails to capitalize on. The irony is that the house collapses around him and he's fine, having gotten only 'a glimpse inside.' The comedic logic is airtight.

jokes.text: I can't look at that. / Unfortunately, Tobias had to.
jokes.setup: The house has collapsed, revealing the inside.
jokes.punchline: Buster can't look at what's inside (implied to be something shocking/lewd from the earlier Gangy/homeless man subplot). Tobias, who wandered to the wrong location looking for Lindsay, is stuck looking at it.
jokes.explanation: The economy of this beat is excellent — 'I can't look at that / Unfortunately, Tobias had to' does a lot with very little. Tobias's wrong-place-wrong-time situation is classic character-based situational comedy.

jokes.text: I can't look at that. / Unfortunately, Tobias had to.
jokes.setup: Buster sees something horrifying inside the collapsed house and looks away.
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes that 'unfortunately, Tobias had to' look — implying whatever Buster saw is so disturbing that Tobias seeing it is worse somehow, or that Tobias is the most unfortunate witness.
jokes.explanation: The 'unfortunately, Tobias had to' is a great economy joke — it implies the horror is real AND that Tobias is somehow especially ill-equipped to witness it. The narrator's sympathy ('unfortunately') for Tobias is deadpan gold.

jokes.text: I got the wrong homeless man. I am sorry. I was looking for Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Tobias has stumbled into the collapsed house scene looking for his wife.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's apology — 'I got the wrong homeless man' — implies he was specifically seeking a homeless man (Lindsay's Tom Jane), which raises more questions than it answers. His calm delivery of this absurd sentence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'I got the wrong homeless man' is a delightful non-sequitur of a line — the specific qualifier 'wrong' implies Tobias had a particular homeless man in mind, which is itself funny. Good economy and character voice.

jokes.text: I got the wrong homeless man. I am sorry. I was looking for Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Tobias appears in the wreckage of the house.
jokes.punchline: Tobias was also looking for a homeless man — presumably having heard about Lindsay's homeless boyfriend — and ended up at the construction site by mistake.
jokes.explanation: Tobias tracking down a homeless man to find his wife is both pathetic and darkly funny. 'I got the wrong homeless man' has a certain bleak comedy. Competent character beat.

jokes.text: You lied to me. You both lied to me. I'm going to war.
jokes.setup: Buster has found out Lucille and Michael were trying to manage his army situation.
jokes.punchline: Buster's declaration that he's 'going to war' as an act of rebellious independence — immediately after the house has collapsed around him — is a hilariously overwrought response to a mild family deception.
jokes.explanation: Buster's overdramatic response is in character, and the idea of a man-child going to war as an act of independence from his mother is genuinely funny. Solid character beat.

jokes.text: I don't agree with your dirty doings here, but I will defend with my life your right to do it.
jokes.setup: Buster is furious but declaring his departure to war.
jokes.punchline: Buster's speech is a garbled paraphrase of Voltaire/First Amendment rhetoric — 'I will defend with my life your right to do it' applied to a Bluth family real estate fraud is absurdly mismatched in grandeur to subject matter.
jokes.explanation: Buster's constitutional-rhetoric speech in this context is perfect character-based absurdism — he's absorbed civic language without any sense of scale. The paraphrase of Voltaire applied to a construction scam is a good incongruity joke.

jokes.text: Buster, you can't! / Yes, I can. I don't agree with your dirty doings here, but I will defend with my life your right to do it.
jokes.setup: Buster declares he's going to war. Lucille protests.
jokes.punchline: Buster's paraphrase of Voltaire — 'I will defend with my life your right to do it' — is hilariously misapplied. He's quoting a principled defense of free speech to justify storming off to a war he's been trying to avoid.
jokes.explanation: The Voltaire quote applied to Buster's petulant march to war is a great highbrow/lowbrow joke. The misapplication is perfect — Buster uses the most dignified possible framing for the least dignified possible circumstance. Strong character integration because Buster's overblown gestures are a hallmark.

jokes.text: Gob charged at Michael with the scissors — but Michael, as he always did, picked rock — which beat scissors.
jokes.setup: The rock-paper-scissors running gag has been set up twice in the episode.
jokes.punchline: In a moment of genuine physical conflict, Michael instinctively 'picks rock' (makes a fist) to defend himself against Gob who is charging with scissors. The narrator frames the self-defense as a rock-paper-scissors victory. The game logic and real-world logic have perfectly merged.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single greatest comedic construction. The rock-paper-scissors gag was set up twice: first with Michael arguing rock should beat everything, then with 'rock sinks boat.' Now, Gob literally attacks Michael with scissors, Michael defends himself with a fist (rock), and the narrator says 'which beat scissors.' Three things happen simultaneously: (1) Michael's opening argument about rock's real-world superiority is validated, (2) the game rule is proven correct in real life, (3) physical self-defense is reframed as winning a children's game. The structural payoff is flawless — this was set up early enough that the audience has almost forgotten it, making the payoff a genuine surprise. Calibrated against the 9.5 anchor: this is comparable to 'Snip, snap!' in its structural elegance and the satisfaction of its long-form setup/payoff. One of the best constructed jokes in Arrested Development history.

jokes.text: Nobody looks good in this. / Especially that little dude. / That's Ann.
jokes.setup: The family is looking at newspaper coverage of the house collapse.
jokes.punchline: Gob refers to Ann as 'that little dude' — not recognizing her as a person of note AND misidentifying her gender. Michael's flat 'That's Ann' continues the running gag.
jokes.explanation: The Ann running gag escalates beautifully — 'Who?' → 'Her?' → 'That little dude.' Each iteration finds a new angle on the family's inability to perceive Ann. The gender misidentification is a new wrinkle that elevates this beyond just a repeat of 'Her?'. Great economy.

jokes.text: Who's— / It doesn't matter.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'That's Ann'; Gob starts to ask who Ann is.
jokes.punchline: Michael preemptively shuts down the question — 'It doesn't matter' — rather than explain Ann again. He's given up. This is the comedic capitulation of the running gag.
jokes.explanation: The 'It doesn't matter' is the perfect end-of-episode beat for the Ann gag — Michael himself has been infected by the family's dismissal of her. The economy is excellent (two words to close a running joke) and it's a slight but genuine character moment: even Michael, who was defending Ann, now can't be bothered.

jokes.text: Dad always said that was your fault.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to get Gob to agree that their fighting is Dad's fault.
jokes.punchline: Gob's response — 'Dad always said that was your fault' — reveals that George Sr. has been playing the brothers against each other. It's a simple line that says everything about George Sr.'s parenting.
jokes.explanation: Eight words that perfectly encapsulate George Sr.'s manipulative parenting. The line is both genuinely funny and genuinely dark — it means George Sr. literally blamed Michael for the brothers' conflict to Gob. The timing, mid-Michael's reconciliation speech, is perfect.

jokes.text: Hello? They hung up. / They hung up on me. / Did I hang up? / I meant to hit speaker. / Oh. / You gotta hit the line first.
jokes.setup: The brothers have just made a genuine emotional connection and agreed not to let their father come between them. The phone rings — it's almost certainly George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Neither brother answers; Gob's fumbling with the phone buttons ('I meant to hit speaker') reveals they both accidentally/intentionally avoid the call, then spend the moment arguing about how to use a phone.
jokes.explanation: The phone fumble works on two levels: comedically (Gob can't work a phone) and emotionally (they both unconsciously avoided their father's call right after saying they would). The rewatch value is in recognizing the emotional dimension of the slapstick.

jokes.text: Hello? They hung up. / They hung up on me. / Hiya, guys. / I couldn't get the money. I don't know what happened.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob not to answer the phone (presumably Dad calling) if he wants their relationship to improve. Gob answers anyway.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. hangs up on Gob. Then Michael, who told Gob not to answer, enters and Gob cheerfully greets him — trying to cover that he just violated their agreement and got immediately punished for it.
jokes.explanation: Gob's immediate betrayal of his implied promise not to take Dad's call — followed by Dad hanging up on him — is good situational comedy. The cheerful 'Hiya, guys' cover is very Gob.

jokes.text: Hello? They hung up. / They hung up on me.
jokes.setup: Michael challenges Gob not to answer the phone call from their father.
jokes.punchline: Gob answers, only for the caller to immediately hang up. He reports this with the same wounded pride he showed when George Sr. called him a horse's ass — 'They hung up on me' as if it's an injustice.
jokes.explanation: Good beat — Gob immediately does the exact thing Michael asked him not to do, and is immediately rejected for it. The 'They hung up on me' as a complaint is in-character wounded Gob.

jokes.text: I couldn't get the money. I don't know what happened. / I'm impressed you hung up. / Did I hang up? I meant to hit speaker.
jokes.setup: Michael congratulates Gob for seemingly hanging up on their father.
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals he thought he hit 'speaker' — he had no idea he hung up. The accident becomes a virtue. Michael's 'I'm impressed you hung up' and then the reveal deflates the brother moment perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The accidental hangup-as-virtue is a nice irony beat that also deflates the sentimental brother moment — Gob's one act of apparent strength was a mistake. Good structural comedy in service of character.

jokes.text: We almost did it though, didn't we? / We almost pulled it off. / No, it wasn't even close. / Yeah, a lot of people got hurt. / This was very, very bad. / Not great. Not great.
jokes.setup: The brothers are reflecting on the episode's events — a collapsed house, a PR disaster.
jokes.punchline: Their attempted positive spin ('We almost did it!') is immediately undercut by reality ('No, it wasn't even close / a lot of people got hurt'). The final 'Not great. Not great.' is a perfect double understatement to close the episode's main action.
jokes.explanation: The double 'Not great. Not great.' as a closing understatement for a catastrophic public failure is a great dry comedy beat. The escalation/deflation rhythm (optimism immediately undercut by reality) is structurally clean and very in-character for both brothers.

jokes.text: Look. You're a really nice chick, but I'm not homeless. / I'm Tom Jane.
jokes.setup: 'On the next Arrested Development' — Tom Jane, the actor, reveals to Lindsay he's not homeless.
jokes.punchline: He says 'I'm Tom Jane' expecting recognition; Lindsay clearly doesn't know who that is. His name-drop lands completely flat.
jokes.explanation: Tom Jane's reveal is the triple-twist completion — homeless man turned out to be movie star, and the movie star's name means nothing to Lindsay. The meta-humor of casting the actual Tom Jane and having him say 'I'm Tom Jane' to someone who doesn't know who he is is a sharp show-business joke. Rewatch bonus for the meta-casting awareness.

jokes.text: Buy me a drink? / You're really sweet, but, no. / I'm Tom Jane.
jokes.setup: Tom Jane has revealed he's not homeless. Lindsay, who rejected him as a 'gross homeless man,' now wants him back — and he rejects her.
jokes.punchline: Tom Jane turns down Lindsay's attempted reversal, then simply restates 'I'm Tom Jane' — presumably thinking this will change her mind. It's a funny loop: the name means nothing to her in either direction.
jokes.explanation: The reversal — Lindsay now pursuing the man she rejected — is a clean irony payoff. Tom Jane restating his name as if it's persuasive is a good final beat on that running joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay, crushed and alone, for the first time misses her husband. Tobias.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been rejected by Tom Jane and is alone.
jokes.punchline: The narrator says Lindsay 'for the first time misses her husband' — the 'for the first time' is the punchline, implying this is a genuinely unprecedented experience for her. Then we cut to Tobias, covered in blue paint.
jokes.explanation: The 'for the first time' qualifier is the entire joke — it says everything about the Tobias/Lindsay marriage. The setup for the blue paint reveal is efficient. Good narrator economy.

jokes.text: Is that George Michael's diamond cream? / Oh, yes, well, I ran out of blue, and I couldn't find anything else. I think my nipples are bleeding though. I don't know if you're supposed to use quite this much.
jokes.setup: Lindsay finds Tobias covered in blue paint (Blue Man Group practice) and then diamond cream.
jokes.punchline: Tobias has used George Michael's diamond cream as blue-paint substitute. His admission that his nipples are bleeding — delivered conversationally — is a perfect Tobias escalation. 'I don't know if you're supposed to use quite this much' implies he used an alarming quantity.
jokes.explanation: A multi-callback joke that elegantly closes the diamond cream/Blue Man Group threads simultaneously. 'My nipples are bleeding' is the Tobias escalation punchline — he's managed to use luxury diamond cream so aggressively that it's caused physical harm. The specific 'I don't know if you're supposed to use quite this much' implies he used an enormous amount, which is funnier than knowing the quantity. Strong character integration and rewatch bonus for tracking all three callback threads.

jokes.text: That guy's a jerk. / No, I am. / No, no, I am.
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias are having a moment of apparent connection after her rejection.
jokes.punchline: Their 'I am / no, I am' exchange about who is 'the jerk' is a twisted romantic gesture — competing to claim fault is the Tobias/Lindsay version of intimacy. It's sweet and sad and funny simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: A gentle character beat — this couple's version of tenderness is competing self-criticism. Functional and character-appropriate, not a standout joke but a nice tonal close.

jokes.text: Because I think I need to go to the hospital right away and see if I can't get some of this diamond dust vacuumed from out of my lungs.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay have had a brief moment of connection.
jokes.punchline: Tobias casually announces he has diamond dust in his lungs — an extremely severe medical situation delivered as an afterthought — and ruins the moment entirely. 'See if I can't get some of this vacuumed from out of my lungs' is beautifully specific about the proposed medical procedure.
jokes.explanation: The perfect closing joke. Tobias has managed to inhale diamond dust — a consequence of applying luxury face cream meant for external use in apparently industrial quantities to his chest — and announces this potential medical emergency with the conversational tone of someone mentioning they need to pick up dry cleaning. 'See if I can't get some of this vacuumed from out of my lungs' is the specific, absurd detail that elevates it: the proposed solution (lung-vacuuming) is almost as funny as the problem. Character integration is near-perfect — only Tobias would create this situation and then describe it this way. Strong earned score because it's the payoff of multiple episode-long threads (diamond cream, Blue Man Group prep, nipple bleeding) converging into one absurd physical crisis.

tmdb_overview: Michael is driven crazy by Gob when he impresses the board as the new company figurehead due to Michael's legal troubles. Meanwhile, George Sr. is arrested in Mexico for a crime Oscar committed, and Buster witnesses something that utterly shocks him.
insight: Housing construction montage sustains 88.9 index through character-driven irony, not punchlines.
